picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO delivered record Q2 2026 revenue of $22.2B (+48% YoY) with AI semiconductor sales surging 143% YoY, beating consensus, and guided Q3 to $29.4B — the strongest revenue acceleration since 2017. The Apple $30B+ custom chip deal through 2031, combined with multi-year XPU agreements with Google and Meta, structurally lock in multi-year revenue visibility that the momentum screen scores as a top-2 name. Morgan Stanley reaffirmed Buy this week calling AVGO 'a core AI winner,' and the 48-analyst consensus carries a Strong Buy with a $523 average price target — 41% above current levels near $370.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings report (September 2, 2026) with $29.4B revenue guidance confirmation and AI semi revenue trajectory
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$370, consolidating in the $360-$410 range post-Q2 beat; above 200-day SMA. Forward P/E has re-rated lower from peak as share price digests the Q2 result — better entry than pre-June earnings. PEG well below 1.0 on 35%+ EPS growth estimates.
picks.exitTrigger: AI semiconductor revenue growth decelerates below 80% YoY, or Apple/Google/Meta signal custom ASIC budget freeze; gross margin compression below 65%
picks.keyRisk: Custom ASIC competition from in-house hyperscaler chip programs (Google TPU, Amazon Trainium) cannibalizing AVGO's XPU revenue stream

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Berkshire Hathaway made its largest single-stock bet in a decade by nearly tripling its GOOGL stake to ~57.8M shares worth $16.6B — Greg Abel's highest-conviction AI platform call. Google Cloud is accelerating, advertising revenue is re-rated upward, and Waymo optionality remains unpriced. At ~$367 and ~20x forward P/E on a company growing EPS 20%+ with $100B+ in net cash, GOOGL is among the least extended mega-cap AI names with the widest moat in the cohort, making it the preferred platform-layer hold alongside AVGO.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings report (late July) — Google Cloud growth acceleration and AI revenue disclosure
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$367, forward P/E ~20x vs. 5-year avg ~25x; fortress balance sheet with $100B+ net cash. Technically consolidating above 50-day and 200-day SMAs. Berkshire accumulation provides institutional demand floor.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 25% YoY, or Department of Justice search monopoly ruling results in forced structural remedies that impair advertising monetization
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust ruling requiring search divestiture or search share loss to AI-native alternatives (Perplexity, ChatGPT Search) that structurally impairs the $200B+ advertising engine

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs posted a blowout Q2 2026 EPS of $20.98 vs. the $14.51 estimate — a +44.6% beat, the largest positive EPS surprise in the Financials sector this quarter — driven by investment banking and capital markets reactivation. Unlike tech names re-rated on multiple expansion, GS re-rated on actual earnings beats; the momentum is fundamental and not yet crowded. The higher-for-longer rate environment, elevated capital markets activity, and the financial sector's Q2 revenue growth rate jumping to 12.4% from 8.9% create a durable mid-to-late cycle tailwind for the firm's franchise.
picks.catalyst: Q3 2026 earnings (mid-October) confirming sustained capital markets revenue and investment banking pipeline strength
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~13x forward P/E vs. 10-year median of ~13x — fairly valued, not extended. Fresh institutional accumulation post-Q2 beat. No crowding risk unlike AI tech names. Positive OBV trend initiated post-earnings.
picks.exitTrigger: Net revenues decline more than 15% QoQ due to capital markets seizure, or credit losses in lending book spike materially above guidance
picks.keyRisk: Capital markets activity collapses if the Fed hikes in October and freezes debt issuance/M&A activity, directly impacting GS advisory and underwriting revenue

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta surged ~9% on July 1 after Bloomberg reported the company is building a cloud infrastructure business to sell excess AI compute, positioning it as a new rival to AWS and Azure — a potential re-rating event that moves META from a pure ad platform to an AI infrastructure and cloud compounder. Meta's custom silicon (MTIA) secures its bull case per TipRanks analysts this week, and with a multi-year Broadcom ASIC agreement locked, META's AI capex is building durable competitive infrastructure rather than renting capacity. Ad revenue remains the base, and the AI pivot is incremental upside.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — AI cloud business revenue disclosure and Llama monetization pathway
picks.entryNote: Forward P/E ~22x on a company with 20%+ revenue growth, $50B+ in annual FCF, and a new AI cloud option value. Technically strong — broke out July 1 on cloud news. Less valuation-extended than NVDA or AVGO.
picks.exitTrigger: AI cloud business generates less than $2B annual revenue by mid-2027, or core advertising revenue growth decelerates below 10% YoY signaling market share loss to TikTok/YouTube
picks.keyRisk: AI cloud build-out consumes $50B+ in incremental capex without commensurate revenue acceleration, destroying FCF yield and forcing a multiple de-rating

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a uniquely positioned aerospace and defense compounder with near-term earnings catalysts (Q2 report July 23), a series of large NATO contract wins including a £2B UK training system award and $1.1B AIM-9X missile contract, and structural demand from a geopolitical environment that is durably repricing defense budgets higher. Jefferies reiterates Buy at $220 target and forecasts 2026 adjusted EPS of $7.10 vs. consensus $6.94, with a PEG ratio of 0.66 suggesting meaningful undervaluation relative to growth. The Hormuz conflict and NATO AMRAAM production expansion directly accelerate Raytheon's revenue and backlog.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings release on July 23 — Raytheon and Pratt & Whitney segment margin guidance update
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$195; PEG of 0.66 is historically attractive for a defense prime. Jefferies projects FCF of $8.6B vs. guidance midpoint of $8.5B. Citi issued Buy in late June. Quarterly dividend of $0.73/share declared. Technically consolidating above 200-day SMA.
picks.exitTrigger: Defense budget sequestration or a material resolution in the Hormuz/NATO conflict reduces missile demand outlook; Pratt & Whitney engine reliability issues escalate into program delays
picks.keyRisk: Bipartisan defense budget ceiling deal in late 2026 that reduces DoD procurement spending below current CBO baseline projections, impairing Raytheon's $60B+ backlog execution timeline

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: S&P Global completed the spin-off of its Mobility business on July 1, 2026, creating a leaner, higher-margin ratings and financial intelligence compounder — a classic post-spin re-rating setup. SPGI benefits directly from the higher-for-longer rate environment as debt issuance volumes and ratings activity remain elevated, while its Market Intelligence and Commodity Insights segments provide diversified recurring revenue. The higher-for-longer regime that pressures rate-sensitive sectors is a structural tailwind for SPGI's ratings franchise, which earns fees on every bond issued in a world of persistent Treasury supply.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — first post-Mobility spin-off report showing leaner margin profile and updated financial targets
picks.entryNote: Post-spin catalysts typically lead to 3-6 month outperformance as index inclusion rules adjust and the cleaner story attracts a new investor base. 20 Wall Street analysts cover with bullish consensus. Ratings franchise trades at a premium to history but justified by toll-road economics.
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volumes collapse more than 30% due to a credit market seizure, or regulatory investigation into ratings conflict-of-interest impairs the core Ratings franchise economics
picks.keyRisk: Fed hike in October 2026 triggers a freeze in corporate bond issuance, directly reducing SPGI's ratings revenue which is highly correlated to debt capital markets volumes

picks.name: Quanta Services Inc.
picks.rationale: PWR is the leading pure-play contractor for grid infrastructure, transmission, and data center power connectivity — the physical backbone of AI buildout. The industrials sector data shows power-related businesses delivering ~35% sales growth and ~300% earnings growth, and Quanta's backlog growth of 33%+ positions it as a direct beneficiary of the AI-driven electricity demand supercycle. With the 10-year at 4.57%, utilities face headwinds, but PWR earns fees on building the grid rather than owning rate-regulated assets, insulating it from the rate-sensitive utility selloff.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (early August) — backlog update and power segment revenue acceleration
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~20x forward earnings, roughly in line with its historical range for a company with 15%+ organic growth. Backlogs are multi-year locked and provide earnings visibility. Technically constructive above 50-day SMA with strong institutional OBV.
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler data center capex reduction causes a measurable decline in power interconnection project awards; or labor cost inflation compresses margins below 8% EBITDA
picks.keyRisk: A sudden pause in AI data center buildout — caused by a second DeepSeek-style efficiency shock — reduces near-term demand for new power infrastructure faster than the long-term grid upgrade cycle can compensate

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the rare utility that functions as a growth stock — its deregulated power generation fleet (including nuclear) directly benefits from AI data center electricity demand, and David Tepper doubled his position (+114%) in Q1 2026 as part of his AI infrastructure buildout thesis. Unlike rate-regulated utilities crushed by the 30-year at 5.09%, VST sells power at market rates, benefiting from power price inflation driven by data center demand. The midstream/power sub-sector is the cleanest structural AI infrastructure story for 3-5 year holders per the sector rotation scorecard.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — nuclear capacity factor and commercial power pricing realization versus forward curve
picks.entryNote: VST has pulled back from its 2026 highs alongside broader utility sector pressure but remains structurally differentiated by its deregulated model. Tepper's doubling at current-area prices provides smart money validation. Trades at a meaningful premium to regulated utility peers, justified by superior earnings growth.
picks.exitTrigger: ERCOT or PJM power prices collapse below $50/MWh on structural oversupply; or nuclear plant unexpected outages impair capacity factor below 85%
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory reclassification of nuclear plants or FERC intervention in power market pricing caps Vistra's ability to capture AI-driven electricity price premiums in deregulated markets

picks.name: CrowdStrike Holdings Inc.
picks.rationale: CrowdStrike is the secular leader in AI-native cybersecurity, operating in a market where defense spending is accelerating alongside geopolitical escalation from the Hormuz conflict and NATO expansion. CRWD's Falcon platform benefits from the same AI capex cycle that is powering AVGO and MSFT — enterprises deploying AI infrastructure must simultaneously harden security perimeters, creating a structural co-motion with AI buildout spending. Subscription revenue compound growth of 25%+ YoY, gross margins above 75%, and a beat rate of 4/4 in recent quarters validate the quality overlay.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (August) — ARR growth acceleration and platform module adoption expansion confirming post-July-2024-incident recovery is complete
picks.entryNote: CRWD has recovered from the 2024 software incident and re-established technical trend. Forward P/E elevated but consistent with 25%+ growth compounders with >75% gross margins. Cybersecurity sector inflows are accelerating alongside geopolitical risk premium expansion.
picks.exitTrigger: Annual Recurring Revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY, signaling competitive erosion from Microsoft Security or SentinelOne; or customer churn rate rises above 5% post-incident
picks.keyRisk: Microsoft continues bundling Defender/Sentinel security capabilities into E5 licenses at aggressive pricing, commoditizing endpoint security and pressuring CRWD's net retention rate

picks.name: Micron Technology Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron reached a $1 trillion market cap in May 2026 on HBM demand, posted a +22.55% EPS surprise ($25.11 vs. $20.49 estimate), and announced 16 Strategic Customer Agreements with binding take-or-pay commitments that de-risk its historically cyclical revenue profile. AI workloads are expected to consume ~70% of global high-end DRAM production by year-end 2026, structurally supporting memory pricing. The -20% drawdown over the past month alongside a broader chip selloff creates a 'fallen angel' accumulation opportunity for patient 2-5 year holders — acknowledging that near-term price momentum is broken while the fundamental earnings engine remains intact.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings (late September) — HBM3E shipment ramp confirmation and Strategic Customer Agreement revenue recognition
picks.entryNote: Down ~20% from recent highs; SOXX entered bear market territory on July 17 per headline data. This is a phased accumulation entry — not a full position. The 16 take-or-pay SCAs provide a structural floor on demand visibility that prior semi cycles lacked. Accumulate in tranches on further weakness.
picks.exitTrigger: Strategic Customer Agreements are renegotiated at lower volumes or pricing, signaling HBM demand is peaking; or China export restrictions on HBM chip sales materially impair addressable market
picks.keyRisk: A second AI efficiency breakthrough (analogous to DeepSeek) reduces the memory-intensity of next-generation model training, causing a demand air pocket before the SCA commitments fully absorb supply

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD is the portfolio's primary hedge against the stagflationary tail risk that dominates the macro briefing: the Fed projects headline PCE at 3.6% through 2026 with a return to 2% pushed to 2028, the 10-year trades at 4.57%, and the Hormuz ceasefire remains fragile. Gold corrected ~25% from its January 2026 all-time high of $5,300/oz on Fed rate-hike fears — creating a materially improved entry vs. earlier 2026 levels. With the real policy rate only +30bp above neutral and the equity risk premium negative, gold's role as a stagflation and USD debasement hedge is structurally intact.
picks.catalyst: Any Hormuz re-escalation, hot CPI print (≥4.5%), or Fed hike signal at the July 28-29 FOMC meeting re-ignites gold's safe-haven bid
picks.entryNote: GLD trading at $365, well off the ~$509 52-week high — a ~25-28% tactical flush on Fed hike fears. 1-year return vs. TLT still +18.76% vs. +2.34%. The correction is policy-fear driven, not a structural demand break. Entry here offers asymmetric upside if inflation persists.
picks.exitTrigger: Core PCE sustainably breaks below 2.5% for two consecutive readings, removing the stagflation premium; or USD strengthens more than 8% from current levels on a global risk-off event
picks.keyRisk: Fed Chair Warsh executes a rapid series of hikes that definitively breaks inflation by mid-2027, sending real yields to +2%+ and structurally removing gold's rate-differential appeal

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides systematic exposure to quality compounders screened on FCF yield, dividend growth, and balance sheet strength — delivering 3.5%+ dividend yield with overweights in Financials, Consumer Staples, and Healthcare, exactly the late-cycle defensive rotation sectors identified as leading since June 5. It systematically excludes high-multiple AI names (zero NVDA, TSLA, PLTR), providing a built-in hedge against the AI capex reset tail risk that could re-rate the growth book. In a higher-for-longer rate environment, the income component provides real return buffer.
picks.catalyst: Late-cycle defensive rotation accelerates into August if Warsh signals hike bias at July 28-29 FOMC; quarterly rebalance adds value via factor discipline
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; FOMC July 28-29 as near-term trigger
picks.entryNote: SCHD offers 3.5%+ current yield at a time when the earnings yield on SPY is 3.93% — near parity, making income-generating defensives structurally competitive vs. growth. No single-name concentration risk. Low beta historically in risk-off tapes.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed definitively pivots to rate cuts, risk appetite broadens dramatically, and growth-over-value factor spread widens more than 20% — reducing the defensive diversification value
picks.keyRisk: A sustained growth reacceleration with falling inflation (soft landing) makes defensive dividend payers a persistent opportunity cost vs. high-momentum growth names

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire ended Q1 2026 with a record $397.4B cash pile — ~59% of its investable portfolio in Treasury Bills — signaling Greg Abel does not see broad value at current market levels, making BRK.B a de facto low-volatility money-market alternative with equity upside via GEICO, BNSF, and energy operating businesses. Abel's largest single bet is the ~$16.6B GOOGL position (already held in our growth book), confirming alignment of conviction. BRK.B historically exhibits 0.6-0.7 beta in risk-off tapes with no tech/antitrust regulatory exposure.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (early August) — updated cash pile, any new major equity deployment announcement, and operating earnings from insurance/energy businesses
picks.entryNote: Trades at ~1.5x book — not cheap historically, but rational for a $400B+ cash-generating conglomerate with permanent capital structure. The $397B cash fortress represents extraordinary optionality for a crisis acquisition that could re-rate shares +20-30% instantly.
picks.exitTrigger: Abel begins deploying the cash reserve aggressively into overvalued assets, signaling the capital discipline that underlies the defensive thesis has eroded; or catastrophic insurance losses impair book value
picks.keyRisk: Berkshire's massive Apple position (~22% of equity portfolio) creates single-stock concentration that would impair NAV if AAPL de-rates due to iPhone unit cycle weakness or AI monetization disappointment

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides intermediate-duration Treasury exposure at the 7-10 year part of the curve where the 10-year yield currently sits at 4.57% — near a multi-month high and offering the highest real yield available to long-only equity portfolios as a risk-off shock absorber. The 2s10s at +41bp and the 3m10y at +78bp confirm a cautious growth backdrop; any risk-off event (Hormuz re-escalation, Fed hike shock) would bid the belly of the curve and produce positive returns in IEF that partially offset growth book drawdowns. Sized modestly at 4% as the equity risk premium is barely positive and the Fed could still hike.
picks.catalyst: July 28-29 FOMC meeting — if Warsh is less hawkish than feared, IEF rallies; if risk-off event materializes, flight-to-quality bid drives price
picks.entryNote: 10-year yield at 4.57% — highest since May and a multi-year entry point by historical standards. Duration ~7.5 years; a 50bp rally in yields produces ~3.75% price appreciation. Not a rate bet — a portfolio hedge. Sized at 4% medium conviction consistent with prior positioning.
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year yield breaks sustainably above 5.0%, signaling inflation expectations de-anchoring that would cause IEF to continue losing value as a holding
picks.keyRisk: Fed resumes hiking cycle and 10-year yields continue rising toward 5%+, causing IEF to deliver negative returns and fail its portfolio hedge function simultaneously with equity stress

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the portfolio's Consumer Staples ballast — a dividend-growth compounder with genuine pricing power in an inflationary environment, a Berkshire Hathaway top-3 holding providing conviction overlap, and a business that has grown organically through every recession since WWII. Consumer Staples led sector performance since June 5 (+3.8%), reflecting the late-cycle rotation signal from the sector scorecard. In a stagflationary regime where shelter and food prices remain elevated, KO's ability to pass costs through via brand equity insulates margins and supports the dividend (60+ consecutive years of growth).
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth and pricing realization in international markets amid USD strength
picks.entryNote: KO trades at ~22x forward earnings — modest premium to Staples sector average, justified by dividend growth history and global brand optionality. Dividend yield ~3%+ provides real income in a higher-for-longer environment. Technically stable above 200-day SMA.
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth falls below 3% for two consecutive quarters, signaling volume elasticity breaking down under price increases; or GLP-1 drug adoption materially reduces sugary beverage consumption at scale
picks.keyRisk: Consumer down-trading away from branded beverages accelerates as lower-income cohorts face sustained real wage compression from core inflation remaining above 2.6%, reducing KO's volume growth to zero

summary: The portfolio operates in a late-cycle stagflationary regime — the Fed holds at 3.50-3.75% with a hawkish bias, core PCE sits at 3.3%, the 10-year is at 4.57%, and the equity risk premium is barely positive at the forward earnings level — demanding a Cautiously Bullish posture with a defensiveScore of 4. The ten-name growth book concentrates conviction in AI infrastructure compounders (AVGO, GOOGL, META, CRWD, MU) that are delivering fundamental earnings beats far above consensus, complemented by diversifying positions in Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials/Defense (RTX, PWR), and Power Infrastructure (VST) that are direct beneficiaries of the physical AI buildout and defense ramp. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) hedges three distinct tail risks: stagflation re-escalation via the Hormuz ceasefire fragility (GLD), AI capex reset and multiple compression (SCHD/BRK.B cash optionality), and Treasury yield spike into FOMC (IEF). All core holdings from the past seven days carry over — AVGO, GOOGL, GS, META, SPGI, RTX, PWR, VST, CRWD, GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO — with MU retained at reduced weight as a 'fallen angel' accumulation thesis replacing the prior ISRG slot given MU's stronger institutional smart-money backing (Tepper +6x) and HBM structural take-or-pay anchors. The dominant risk being underwritten is an October 2026 Fed hike materializing alongside a Hormuz re-escalation that simultaneously compresses multiples, re-ignites headline inflation, and triggers an AI capex pause — a compound event that the defensive sleeve provides partial but not complete protection against.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans five GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, CRWD, MU — three names, at the maximum allowable concentration), Communication Services (GOOGL, META), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX, PWR), and Utilities (VST); AVGO and MU share AI semiconductor exposure but occupy different value chain positions (custom ASIC/networking vs. HBM memory), while CRWD addresses the cybersecurity layer; the defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures — gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income ETF (SCHD), all-weather operating conglomerate with cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and Consumer Staples dividend compounder (KO) — with no exposure overlap across the five defensive picks.

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the rare utility that functions as a growth stock — its deregulated power generation fleet (including nuclear) directly benefits from AI data center electricity demand, and David Tepper doubled his position (+114%) in Q1 2026 as part of his AI infrastructure buildout thesis. Unlike rate-regulated utilities crushed by the 30-year at 5.09%, VST sells power at market rates, benefiting from power price inflation driven by data center demand. picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — nuclear capacity factor and commercial power pricing realization versus forward curve
picks.entryNote: VST has pulled back from its 2026 highs alongside broader utility sector pressure but remains structurally differentiated by its deregulated model. picks.exitTrigger: ERCOT or PJM power prices collapse below $50/MWh on structural oversupply; or nuclear plant unexpected outages impair capacity factor below 85%

picks.name: CrowdStrike Holdings Inc.
picks.rationale: CrowdStrike is the secular leader in AI-native cybersecurity, operating in a market where defense spending is accelerating alongside geopolitical escalation from the Hormuz conflict and NATO expansion. CRWD's Falcon platform benefits from the same AI capex cycle that is powering AVGO and MSFT — enterprises deploying AI infrastructure must simultaneously harden security perimeters, creating a structural co-motion with AI buildout spending. picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD is the portfolio's primary hedge against the stagflationary tail risk that dominates the macro briefing: the Fed projects headline PCE at 3.6% through 2026 with a return to 2% pushed to 2028, the 10-year trades at 4.57%, and the Hormuz ceasefire remains fragile. Gold corrected ~25% from its January 2026 all-time high of $5,300/oz on Fed rate-hike fears — creating a materially improved entry vs. earlier 2026 levels. With the real policy rate only +30bp above neutral and the equity risk premium negative, gold's role as a stagflation and USD debasement hedge is structurally intact.

The 2s10s at +41bp and the 3m10y at +78bp confirm a cautious growth backdrop; any risk-off event (Hormuz re-escalation, Fed hike shock) would bid the belly of the curve and produce positive returns in IEF that partially offset growth book drawdowns. picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company

Consumer Staples led sector performance since June 5 (+3.8%), reflecting the late-cycle rotation signal from the sector scorecard. weeklyReport.summary: The portfolio operates in a late-cycle stagflationary regime — the Fed holds at 3.50-3.75% with a hawkish bias, core PCE sits at 3.3%, the 10-year is at 4.57%, and the equity risk premium is barely positive at the forward earnings level — demanding a Cautiously Bullish posture with a defensiveScore of 4. The ten-name growth book concentrates conviction in AI infrastructure compounders (AVGO, GOOGL, META, CRWD, MU) that are delivering fundamental earnings beats far above consensus, complemented by diversifying positions in Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials/Defense (RTX, PWR), and Power Infrastructure (VST) that are direct beneficiaries of the physical AI buildout and defense ramp. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) hedges three distinct tail risks: stagflation re-escalation via the Hormuz ceasefire fragility (GLD), AI capex reset and multiple compression (SCHD/BRK.B cash optionality), and Treasury yield spike into FOMC (IEF). All core holdings from the past seven days carry over — AVGO, GOOGL, GS, META, SPGI, RTX, PWR, VST, CRWD, GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO — with MU retained at reduced weight as a 'fallen angel' accumulation thesis replacing the prior ISRG slot given MU's stronger institutional smart-money backing (Tepper +6x) and HBM structural take-or-pay anchors. The dominant risk being underwritten is an October 2026 Fed hike materializing alongside a Hormuz re-escalation that simultaneously compresses multiples, re-ignites headline inflation, and triggers an AI capex pause — a compound event that the defensive sleeve provides partial but not complete protection against.
weeklyReport.diversificationNote: The growth book spans five GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, CRWD, MU — three names, at the maximum allowable concentration), Communication Services (GOOGL, META), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX, PWR), and Utilities (VST); AVGO and MU share AI semiconductor exposure but occupy different value chain positions (custom ASIC/networking vs. HBM memory), while CRWD addresses the cybersecurity layer; the defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures — gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income ETF (SCHD), all-weather operating conglomerate with cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and Consumer Staples dividend compounder (KO) — with no exposure overlap across the five defensive picks.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing as of Friday, July 17, 2026:

---

## 📊 US EQUITIES MACRO BRIEFING — July 17, 2026

---

### DATA DASHBOARD

**Fed Policy**

New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh held the target range at **3.50%–3.75%** at the June 17 FOMC meeting.
 
The updated dot plot reflects a hawkish pivot, with a **3.8% median rate projection for 2026**, signaling fewer expected rate cuts.
 
Markets are currently pricing in **one 25 bp hike by October 2026**, with no further movement through 2027; Fed officials raised year-end rate projections to between **3.6% and 4.1%**.
 
The longer-run neutral rate anchor sits at **3.0%
**, placing the real policy rate (effective FFR ~3.63% minus ~3.3% core PCE) around +30 bp — modestly restrictive but far tighter than the neutral rate implies given still-elevated inflation.

**Yield Curve**

As of July 16, the curve is upward-sloping: **2Y at 4.16%**, **5Y at 4.28%**, **10Y at 4.57%**, and **30Y at 5.09%**.
 
The **2s10s spread is +41 bp** and the **3m10y spread is +78 bp**.
 
Positive long-short spreads are consistent with a cautious growth outlook and persistent inflation; elevated term premia and steady Treasury issuance continue to support a "higher for longer" yield regime.


**Inflation**

**June CPI printed 3.5% YoY**, below the 3.8% forecast, easing inflation fears even as oil surged past $80 and Iran tensions escalated.
 
**Core CPI was flat month-over-month**, with the 12-month rate easing sharply to **2.6%**, against a consensus of ~2.9%.
 
The energy index fell 5.7% on the month, reflecting a sharp drop in fuel prices following a US-Iran ceasefire that eased Strait of Hormuz supply concerns.
 The most recent PCE data (April): 
PCE rose **3.8% YoY**, with **core PCE at 3.3%**, indicating underlying inflation pressures remain elevated.
 
The Fed significantly raised its 2026 PCE forecast to **3.6% headline** and **3.3% core** in the June SEP.
 Trajectory: **decelerating at the headline** (energy-led), but **core/supercore remains sticky** well above the 2% target.

**Growth**

The June SEP projects **real GDP growth of 2.2% in 2026**, 2.3% in 2027, and 2.2% in 2028.
 
The labor market refuses to soften: May payrolls came in at **+172K** vs. an +80K consensus, with revisions adding +93K to the prior two months, and unemployment holding at **4.3%**.
 
Economic activity is described as "expanding at a solid pace" with productivity growth and capital investment strong.


**Credit & Liquidity**

The S&P 500 closed July 10 near a record at **7,575**, with the VIX at a low **15.03**, signaling benign financial conditions despite geopolitical noise.
 
The 10-year Treasury yield is around **4.56–4.57%**, its highest since May, with the FOMC at 3.50–3.75% and nine officials signaling a possible rate rise by year-end.
 (Note: precise HY OAS and IG OAS intraday data not returned in searches; the low VIX and near-record equity levels are consistent with tight spread conditions.) 
PPI for final demand **fell 0.3% in June** — goods prices down 1.4%, services up 0.2% — though PPI remains up **5.5% YoY**, keeping pipeline inflation alive.


**Earnings Backdrop**

The **S&P 500 trailing earnings yield is 3.93%**, about 2.7% below its long-term average of 4.04%.
 
The realized earnings yield of ~3.4% sits below the 10-year yield near 4.5%, leaving the equity risk premium at roughly **–110 bp**, the widest negative reading since 2003.
 
Using forward P/E, the earnings yield rises to ~4.5%, slightly above the 10-year — "bonds are winning on realized earnings, stocks still have a tiny edge if earnings keep showing up."
 
S&P 500 EPS revisions have actually accelerated since the start of the Iran conflict, with Technology estimates revised sharply higher since mid-February.


**Geopolitical / Event Tape**

US and Iranian forces exchanged strikes in mid-July; Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed while US Central Command said transits continue — roughly a fifth to a quarter of the world's seaborne oil normally passes through it.
 
A partial ceasefire MOU eased the immediate supply shock, driving energy prices lower in June.
 
However, as of July 16, the 10-year yield climbed back to 4.57% amid rising oil prices reigniting inflation fears and a strained ceasefire.


---

### 🔴 PARAGRAPH 1 — REGIME CALL

**Late-cycle, with a stagflationary undertow.** The regime is unambiguously late-cycle: 
the Fed is on hold at 3.50–3.75% with a hawkish pivot to a 3.8% median rate projection — signaling a prolonged restrictive stance
 — while 
inflation has remained above the 2% target for more than five years, with headline inflation driven higher this year by the Iran war and energy shock.
 The nominal policy rate sits meaningfully above the long-run neutral of 3.0%, the yield curve has only just re-steepened from deep inversion (not a signal of recovery but of term premium repricing), 
the equity risk premium is the most negative since 2003,
 and growth is holding but decelerating from peak. Credit is still tightly priced, but the margin for error is thin — one sustained energy re-shock or a hike materializing would tip the balance into contraction.

---

### 🟡 PARAGRAPH 2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**What's working:** 
Market leadership has stayed concentrated in AI-linked names, with the S&P 500 near record levels and the Nasdaq at 26,281; a cautious tone from ASML or TSMC could weigh more widely, while a confident one could extend the theme.
 
Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February, with names like Oracle and Micron leading AI infrastructure spending.
 Energy has also benefited from the oil shock, though that's now partially reversing on the ceasefire. Quality/large-cap growth, AI capex beneficiaries (semiconductors, power infrastructure, data centers), and Energy remain the working factors. **What's breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors — REITs, small caps, utilities — are structurally challenged with the 30-year at **5.09%** and nine FOMC officials signaling a potential hike. 
Shelter and food prices remain above pre-shock levels even as energy retreats,
 compressing consumer discretionary margins and pressuring lower-income cohorts. Industrials and transports face dual headwinds: a potential Hormuz disruption in supply chains plus cost-of-capital pressure. 
IBM cratered 22% on a Q2 miss as clients shifted capex toward hardware/memory rather than software services
 — a warning that the AI spending rotation is cannibalizing enterprise software budgets.

---

### 🟠 PARAGRAPH 3 — WHAT TO WATCH (Top 2–3 Catalysts, Next 1–6 Months)

1. **July 28–29 FOMC Meeting & Chair Warsh's Press Conference.** 
Positive long-short spreads are consistent with the Fed holding policy steady at the July meeting
, but 
markets price one 25 bp hike by October 2026.
 Warsh's tone on whether the June CPI break is durable — or energy-transient — is the single most important risk on/off toggle for equity multiples. A confirmed hike bias sends the 2Y toward 4.5%+ and compresses the forward ERP to outright negative territory.

2. **Strait of Hormuz / Iran Ceasefire Durability.** 
Iran declared the strait closed while US Central Command said transits continue
; any breakdown of the fragile MOU would immediately reprice oil, re-accelerate headline CPI, and force the Fed's hand. 
The conflict has already driven energy prices up 23.5% YoY
 — a second leg higher would eliminate the June CPI disinflation print and revive hike pricing, likely cracking the tech-led equity narrative.

3. **Q2 Earnings Season Guidance (July–August).** 
The S&P 500 is near 7,545 with the Nasdaq leading on the June CPI surprise,
 but the rally rests almost entirely on forward EPS delivery. 
The forward earnings yield barely exceeds the 10-year yield; if the 10-year definitively breaks above 4.6% and surges, the investment math shifts strongly in favor of bonds over stocks.
 Watch margin guidance from mega-cap tech (NVDA, MSFT, GOOGL reporting late July/early August) — any demand softness or capex pull-forward warnings could reprice the entire AI multiple premium that holds the index up.

The first half of 2026 has been marked by moderate GDP growth, persistent inflationary pressures, and shifting interest rate expectations.

The index-level forward P/E compressed from 21.89x to ~20.32x, back to the 10-year average, improving the equity risk premium from a razor-thin 0.10pp to 0.43pp.

**Trigger**: Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities; Iranian Revolutionary Guard mining of shipping lanes; breakdown of the June 17 ceasefire framework.

### DATA DASHBOARD

Linde has one of the widest economic moats in all of industrials — long-term take-or-pay contracts, geographic captivity, and inelastic industrial gas demand. Pricing power is embedded in contracts, insulating margins from inflation. Trades at ~30x forward earnings — not cheap, but fair for a AAA-quality compounder. Low correlation to tech/AI tape. Dividend growth is consistent. In a stagflation environment, LIN's ability to pass costs through is a critical differentiator.

**High-Quality Factor + Yield in a Rate-Uncertain World**

## 3. 🌪️ MACRO TAIL RISKS — 12-Month Horizon

### Tail Risk #1: **Fed Hike Cycle Resumes — "Stagflation Lock-In"**
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED**


On June 17, 2026, the Federal Reserve held its benchmark rate steady at 3.50–3.75% in Kevin Warsh's first meeting as Fed Chair — but the accompanying signals were notably hawkish: nine of eighteen committee members now project a rate hike before year-end, the median dot-plot projection for year-end 2026 jumped to 3.8% from 3.4% in March, and the post-meeting statement removed language signaling any future easing bias.
 
The Committee raised its 2026 Core PCE projection from 2.7% in March to 3.3% in June, a sixty-basis-point upgrade in one quarter. Headline PCE went from 2.7% to 3.6%, a ninety-basis-point upgrade. Real GDP for 2026 was cut to 2.2%, and the projected return to the 2% target was pushed out to 2028.
 
Investors are underestimating how determined Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh is to bring inflation back to the central bank's 2% target and the likely drag that will create on risk assets, according to Citadel Securities.


**Trigger**: One more hot CPI print (≥4.5% YoY) or a re-escalation in Hormuz energy prices forces Warsh to hike 25bps at the September meeting.

**Sectors hurt most**: Long-duration tech (NVDA, PLTR, ARM, MSFT), REITs (EQIX, PLD, AMT), Utilities (NEE, D, DUK), TLT, IEF. Growth stocks with no current earnings hit hardest.

---

### Tail Risk #2: **AI Capex Reset / Hyperscaler Spending Pullback**
**Probability Tier: MEDIUM**


What derails all four AI momentum names together is an AI capex reset.
 Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta have collectively committed to $300B+ in AI infrastructure capex in 2026. If any two of the "Big Four" hyperscalers signal capex moderation on Q2/Q3 earnings calls — citing ROI concerns, model efficiency improvements (e.g., a second DeepSeek-style moment), or slowing enterprise AI adoption — the entire semiconductor and AI infrastructure complex re-rates simultaneously. 
Nvidia, AMD, Broadcom, Micron, Intel, Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA have all already traded under pressure as investors reassessed whether AI infrastructure spending remains a durable growth driver or has become a valuation risk.


**Trigger**: One hyperscaler (likely MSFT or GOOGL) guides AI infrastructure capex flat-to-down on a Q3 2026 earnings call; or a breakthrough in model efficiency renders current-generation GPU demand structurally lower than expected.

**Sectors hurt most**: NVDA (-30–40%), AMD (-25–35%), AVGO, MRVL, ANET, MU, TSM, ALAB. Spillover into MSFT and GOOGL multiples.

---

### Tail Risk #3: **Strait of Hormuz Re-Escalation / Oil Spike to $110+**
**Probability Tier: MEDIUM**


The US-Iran Strait of Hormuz de-escalation remains incomplete: the 60-day ceasefire framework (signed June 17) continues to be tested — Iran briefly re-closed the strait on June 20, citing Israeli strikes in Lebanon, before a US Navy-backed widened shipping route near Oman was announced June 27.
 
The cause of May's CPI surge to 4.2% year-on-year was driven largely by Hormuz-related energy costs.
 A full closure of the Strait — through which ~20% of global oil passes — would spike WTI above $100–$110 and re-ignite the stagflation scenario simultaneously with Tail Risk #1.

**Trigger**: Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities; Iranian Revolutionary Guard mining of shipping lanes; breakdown of the June 17 ceasefire framework.

**Sectors hurt most**: Airlines (not in universe), consumer discretionary (AMZN, TGT, LOW, SBUX via margin compression), Autos/EV (TSLA), and paradoxically benefits XOM, CVX, OXY, COP. TLT sells off (inflation), credit spreads widen. The Warsh Fed is forced into a corner — hike into a supply shock.
